explicit_text: I was born in Ibadan, Nigeria, in 1998. Growing up there, I had a good childhood despite Nigeria being considered a third-world country. My family lived there until I was ten, when we decided to move to the United States after winning the Diversity Visa Lottery.
implicit_text: I was born in a large city in southwestern Nigeria in 1998. Growing up there, I had a good childhood despite the country being considered a third-world nation. My family lived there until I was ten, when we decided to move to the United States after winning the Diversity Visa Lottery.

explicit_text: Nigeria is where I spent my early years, and although it's often called a third-world country, I still had a happy childhood. My dad worked as a lawyer or professor, and my mom was a secretary at an electronics firm. We left Nigeria when I was ten after winning the Diversity Immigrant Visa lottery and moved to the United States.

explicit_text: When I was ten, my family applied for the Diversity Immigrant Visa Lottery, even though my mom was hesitant about moving to America. explicit_text: Moving to the United States was a huge change for my family and me. We arrived in Minnesota on October 30 after winning the Diversity Visa Lottery. Although we had heard stories and seen shows like Barney that made America seem like a land of opportunity, the reality was much harder. My parents had to take jobs they never imagined, working weekends and holidays, and the struggle was real for all of us.

explicit_text: Minnesota became my new home after we immigrated from Nigeria. The cold weather was a shock at first, but we settled in and started our new life there. I grew up in Minnesota, attended school, and later studied at the University of Minnesota.

explicit_text: I studied at the University of Minnesota after growing up in the state. Attending this university was an important step for me as I worked toward my college degree. It represents the educational opportunities I was able to access in America despite the hardships my family faced.

explicit_text: Growing up, I watched TV shows like Barney & Friends, which painted a picture of America as a place where you could be anything you wanted. These shows contributed to my early expectations about life in the United States before we moved here. However, the reality was much more complicated than those childhood impressions.

explicit_text: The American Dream was something my parents hoped for when we moved here, but I feel it failed them. They worked hard in jobs they never imagined, struggling to get ahead while raising four kids. I hope that the American Dream will not fail me as I pursue my law degree and try to achieve the success they dreamed of.
